The issue arises from the logic used to:
Correct Padding Calculation
When calculating the padding, the function should consider the case where the error is at the end of the line. This can be achieved by adjusting the loop condition to include the character number even if it equals the length of the source excerpt. Additionally, the function should append spaces for non-whitespace characters and the correct whitespace characters from the source excerpt. This adjustment ensures that the arrow is correctly aligned with the error location, even at the end of a line.

Handle End-of-Line Errors
Introduce a specific condition to handle cases where the error is at the end of the line. In such cases, the function can append the necessary padding based on the length of the source excerpt and the character number, ensuring the arrow points correctly to the end of the line. This could involve appending the length of the source excerpt minus one in spaces, followed by the arrow, to indicate the error location accurately.

Simplify Padding Logic
Rethink the padding logic to simplify the appending of whitespace and non-whitespace characters. This could involve using a uniform method to calculate the padding, such as using the minimum of the character number and the length of the source excerpt minus one, to avoid index out-of-bounds errors. By simplifying the logic, the function can correctly handle both within-line and end-of-line error locations without the need for complex conditional statements, making the code more robust and easier to maintain.
